Join my team! We’re hiring a QC / QA Analyst (Geospatial)🌍


We’re looking for someone with experience in geospatial analysis to support our team on a 3-month full-time freelance contract, with a strong possibility of extension.

This role focuses on analyzing over-ocean optical, SAR, and potentially hyperspectral imagery to ensure high-quality data for our AI/ML models. Your work will have a direct impact on model performance and product quality.


🔍 Key responsibilities:

·      Review and analyze remote sensing imagery for quality assurance

·      Identify and correct errors or inconsistencies in imagery data

·      Collaborate with AI/ML teams to meet data quality requirements

·      Follow standard procedures for image processing and validation

·      Document findings and clearly communicate any issues


✅ Must have:

·      Experience with GIS (preferably QGIS)

·      NATO passport

·      Ability to work remotely and independently

·      Strong attention to detail (this role involves repetitive QA tasks)


📄 Contract: Freelance

 ⏳ Duration: 3 months (full-time of part-time, with possible extension)


If you’re detail-oriented and passionate about geospatial data and AI, send me a message!

Shahid Hasan is an experienced technology leader specializing in AI-enabled agile delivery, data intelligence, and enterprise transformation. Currently serving as a Senior Scrum Master at Hiring Boots | Recruitment Consultancy & Contract Staffing, he leads high-performing teams using Scrum methodologies while integrating AI-driven insights into planning, execution, and human resource optimization. Previously, as a Project Manager Scrum Master at BigData Republic Dimension Labs, he worked extensively on data modeling, data warehousing, and analytics-driven projects, enabling organizations to leverage structured data for smarter decision-making. His strong command of agile frameworks, combined with data-centric thinking, positions him as a key driver of digital and AI-led transformation initiatives.

Earlier in his career, Shahid held leadership roles including Director at COOLWAVE, where he focused on business leadership, ERP systems like Odoo, and operational scalability. At IBM, he worked as a Business Intelligence Consultant, building data warehousing solutions and enabling enterprise reporting capabilities. He also served as a Project Lead at the Ministry of Education, Saudi Arabia, contributing to large-scale government technology initiatives, and as a Technical Lead/Senior Software Engineer at NIIT Technologies, where he built strong foundations in Informatica, data warehousing, and enterprise systems. Across his journey, Shahid combines agile expertise, data engineering knowledge, and emerging AI applications to deliver impactful, scalable solutions.

You keep hearing 'RAG' in every AI demo.

But nobody explains why it matters for finance.

Here's the simplest way to understand it:

Imagine hiring a brilliant analyst with encyclopedic general knowledge. That's your LLM.

But they started yesterday. They don't know your policies, your data, your context.

Now give them access to your internal document library.

Before answering any question, they search your documents, find what's relevant, then generate their answer based on what they found—citing specific sources.

That's RAG. Retrieval first. Generation second.

Why this transforms finance operations:

— Compliance queries get answered in plain language with source references instead of 500-page manual searches
— Credit memos drafted using your institution's actual frameworks, not generic templates
— Regulatory gaps identified by comparing requirements against current procedures
— New hires onboarded with current documentation, not outdated slides
— Contract deviations flagged automatically against your standard terms

The critical advantage?

RAG reduces hallucination because answers come from retrieved documents, not just training data. It tells you not only the answer but where in your documentation that answer lives.

Essential in regulated environments.

Three honest caveats:

1. Retrieval can miss poorly structured documents. The AI can misinterpret what it finds. Outdated policies = confidently wrong answers.
2. Human review remains non-negotiable.
3. But for financial institutions sitting on mountains of searchable-in-theory documentation that nobody can actually search—and that's most of them—this is one of the highest-ROI AI investments available.

Which finance use case would benefit most from instant document retrieval in your organization?

🌍 Location: Remote

✔️ Working with production-level ML codebases

✔️ Preparing datasets, features & benchmarking metrics
✔️ Debugging and improving real-world ML systems

LLMs | AI evaluation | benchmarking systems
🚀 Real talk:
If you want to work on how AI models are tested, improved, and pushed to production quality — this is a serious opportunity.

Legal problems in tech companies do not begin when something has already gone wrong. They begin much earlier during growth, hiring, product scaling, and entry into new markets.

If you look at the market more broadly, the same pressure points keep repeating: ownership of the product, data and AI, team and contracts, as well as corporate structure and transactions. According to a survey of 150+ companies, 34% face difficulties proving ownership of their intellectual property, while only 31% have in-house specialists dealing with these issues. At the same time, outsourcing of IP protection continues to grow.

A separate risk area is data, platform rules, and AI. In its 2025 outlook, techUK identifies online safety, AI, digital markets, privacy, and the growing number of disputes around data and information authenticity as key issues for the tech sector. In other words, for a technology company, this is no longer something that sits somewhere on the edge of legal work, it is part of the business model itself.

Team structure and the contractual layer are another chronic source of weakness. LegalVision lists IP, team relationships, compliance, and information protection among the most common legal risks for tech businesses. This shows quite clearly that the problem is usually not one document in isolation, but the overall way the business is legally structured.

For me, the main conclusion is simple: tech companies most often lose resilience not because of the technology itself, but because of the weak legal architecture built around it. When product ownership is not fully consolidated, the team is poorly documented, the data framework is not properly structured, and the company’s growth structure has not been thought through, law begins to directly affect speed, transactions, and scaling.

That is why legal work for a tech company is no longer about paperwork. It is about control, growth, and protection.

#techlaw #startup #dataprivacy #aicompliance #techcompanies #ventures
